title: 第 56 節
en: (5) Conclusion (Nigamana), therefore it has fire V In both cases the process of inference is the same, but the second is supposed to be more rhetorical, more persuasive, and therefore more useful in controversy.
What is called by Annambha^a the conclusion for oneself, corresponds totidem verbis to the first form of Aristotle's syllogism: —
All that smokes is fiery, The mountain smokes;
Therefore the mountain is fiery.

We must not forget, however, that whatever there is of formal Logic in these short extracts, has but one object with Gotama, that of describing knowledge as one of the qualities of the Self, and as this knowledge is not confined to sensuous perceptions, Gotama felt it incumbent on him to explain the nature and prove the legitimacy of the inferential kind of knowledge also. It is not so much logic 1 Nyaya-Sutras I, 32.
Vaiseshika-Sutras IX, 2, 4.
8 The Yaiseshika terms are (i) Pratigrna, (a) Apadesa, (3) Nidarsana. (4) Amisawdhana, (5) Pratyamnaya.

ANUMANA FOB OTHERS. 433 as it is noetic that interested Ka^ada. He was clearly aware of the inseparability of inductive and deductive reasoning. The formal logician, from the time of Aristotle to our own, takes a purely technical interest in the machinery of the human mind, he collects, he arranges and analyses the functions of our reasoning faculties, as they fall under his observation. But the question which occupies Gotama is, How it is that we know any thing which we do not, nay which we cannot perceive by our senses, in fact, how we can justify inferential knowledge. From this point of view we can easily see that neither induction nor deduction, if taken by itself, would be sufficient for him. Deductive reasoning may in itself be most useful for forming Vyaptis, it may give a variety of different aspects to our knowledge, but it can never add to it. And if on one side Gotama cannot use deduction, because it teaches nothing new, he cannot on the other rely entirely on induction, because it cannot teach anything certain or unconditional.

The only object of all knowledge, according to Gotama, is absolute truth or Prama. He knew as well as Aristotle that €7rayo)y?? in order to prove the oAco? must be 8ia irdvrcov, and that this is impossible. Knowledge gained by epagogic reasoning is, strictly speaking, always tirl TO iro'Av, and not what Gotama would call Prama. The conclusion, f. 1, at which Aristotle arrives by way of induction, that animals with little bile are long-lived, might be called a Vyapti, He arrives at it by saying that man, horse, and mule (C) are long-lived (A) ; man, horse, and mule (C) have little bile (B); therefore all animals with little bile are long-lived. Gotama does not differ much from this, but he would express himself in a different way. He would say, wherever we see the attribute of little bile, we also see the attribute of long life, as for instance in men, horses, mules, &c. But there he would not stop. He would value this Vyapti merely as a means of establishing a new rule; he would use it as a means of deduction and say, 'Now we know that the elephant has little bile, therefore we know ttlso that he is long-lived/ Or to use another instance, where Aristotle says that all men are mortal, KanMa 434 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

would say that humanity is pervaded by mortality, or that we have never seen humanity without mortality; and where Aristotle concludes that kings are mortal because they belong to the class of men, Gotama, if he argued for himself only, and not for others, would say that kinghood is pervaded by manhood and manhood by mortality, and therefore kings are mortal.

It would be easy to bring objections against this kind of reasoning, and we shall see that Indian philosophers themselves have not been slow in bringing them forward, and likewise in answering them. One thing can be said in favour of the Indian method. If we go on accumulating instances to form an induction, if, as in the afore-mentioned case, we add horses, mules, men, and the like, we approximate no doubt more and more to a general rule, but we never eliminate all real, much less all possible, exceptions. The Hindu, on the contrary, by saying, ' Wherever we have seen the attribute of little bile, we have observed long life/ or better still, ' We have never observed long life without the attribute of little bile/ and by then giving a number of mere instances, and these by way of illustration only, excludes the reality, though not the possibility, of exceptions. He states, as a fact, that wherever the one has been, the other has been seen likewise, and thus throws the onus probandi as to any case to the contrary upon the other side. The Hindu knows the nature of induction quite well enough to say in the very words of European philosophers, that because in ninety-nine cases a Vyapti l or rule has happened to be true, it does not follow that it will be so in the hundredth case. If it can be proved, however, that there never has been an instance where smoke was seen without fire, the mutual inherence and inseparable connection of smoke and fire is more firmly established than it would be by any number of accumulated actual instances where the two have been seen together.
The conditions (Upadhis) under which it is allowable to form a Vyapti, that is to say, to form a universal rule, have greatly occupied the thoughts of Hindu philosophers.
1 'Satasa/i saha&aritayor api vyabliiAaropalabdhefe.* Anumanakhanrfa of Tattvafrintamam.

ANUMANA FOR OTHERS. 435 Volumes after volumes have been written on the subject, and though they may not throw any new light on the origin of universal®, they furnish at all events a curious parallel to the endeavours of European philosophers in defence both of inductive and deductive thinking.

It seems hardly time as yet to begin to criticise the inductive and the deductive methods as elaborated by Hindu philosophers. We must first know them more fully. Such objections as have hitherto been started were certainly not unknown to Gotama and Kanada themselves. In accordance with their system of Pftrvapaksha and Uttarapaksha, every conceivable objection was started by them and carefully analysed and answered. Thus it has been pointed out by European philosophers that the proposition that wherever there is smoke there is fire, would really lose its universal character1 by the introduction of the instance, ' as on the kitchen hearth/ But the Hindu logicians also were perfectly aware of the fact that this instance is not essential to a syllogism. They look upon the instance simply as a helpful reminder for controversial purposes, as an illustration to assist the memory, not as an -essential part of the process of the proof itself. It is meant to remind us that we must look out for a Vyapti between the smoke which we see, and the fire which is implied, but not seen. It is therefore in rhetorical syllogisms or syllogisms for others only that the instance has its proper place. In Sfttra I, 35 Gotama says, ( The third member or example is some familiar case of the fact which, through its having a character which is invariably attended by that which is to be established, establishes (in conjunction with the reason) the existence of that character which is to be established/ It is Indian rhetoric therefore far more than Indian logic that is responsible for the introduction of this third member which contains the objectionable instance; and rhetoric, though it is not logic, yet, as Whately says, is an offshoot of logic.

1 Ritter, History of Philosophy, IV, p. 365,, says that 'two members of Kanada's argument are evidently superfluous, while, by the introduction of an example in the third, the universality of the conclusion is vitiated/ F f 2 436 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

The fact is that Gotama cares far more for the formation of a Vy&pti, pervasion, than for the manner in which it may serve hereafter as the basis of a syllogism, which must depend on the character of the Vyapti. A Vy&pti was considered as threefold in the school of Gotama, as Anvayavyatireki, Keval&nvayi, and Kevala-vyatirekl The first, the Anvaya-vyatireki, present and absent, is illustrated by such a case as, Where there is smoke, there is fire, and where fire is not, smoke is not. The second, or Kevalanvayi, i.e. present only, is illustrated by such a case as, Whatever is cognisable is nameable, where it is impossible to bring forward anything that is not cognisable. The third case, or Kevala-vyatireki, is illustrated by a case such as, Earth is different from the other elements, because it is odorous. Here we could not go on and say, all that is different from the other elements has odour, because the only case in point (Ud^haraTza) would again be earth. But we have to say, what is not different from the other elements is not odorous, as water (by itself). But this earth is not so, is not inodorous, and therefore it is not not-different from the other elements, but different from them, q.e.d.

Much attention has also been paid by Hindu philosophers to the working of the UpMhis or conditions assigned to a Vy&pti. Thus in the ordinary Vy&pti that there is smoke in a mountain, because there is fire, the presence of wet fuel was an Up&dhi, or indispensable condition. This Up&dhi pervades what is to be established (S&dhya-vy&paka), in this case, fire, but it does not pervade what establishes (SMhana-vyapaka), i.e. smoke, because fire is not pervaded by or invariably accompanied by wet fuel, as, for instance, in the case of a red-hot iron ball, where we have really fire without smoke. Hence it would not follow by necessity that there is fire because there is smoke, or that there is no fire because there is no smoke. How far the Indian mind may go in these minutiae of reasoning may be seen from the following instance given by Dr. Ballantyne in his Lectures on the Ny&ya-philosophy, founded chiefly on the Tarkasamgraha, p. 59 : —

' To be the constant accompanier of what is to be esta- FOE OTHERS. 437 Wished (SMhya-vyapakatva) consists in the not being the (counter- entity (Apratiyogitva) of any absolute non-existence (Atyantabhava) having the same subject of inhesion (SamanadhikaraTza) as that which is to be established. To be not the constant accompanier of the argument (Sadhanavyapakatva) consists in the being the counterentity (Pratiyogitva) of some absolute non-existence [not impossibly] resident in that which possesses [the character tendered as an] argument.'

The credit of this translation belongs not to me, but to the late Dr. Ballantyne, who was assisted in unravelling these cobwebs of Nyaya logic by the Nyaya-Pandits of the Sanskrit College at Benares. Such native aid would seem to be almost indispensable for such an achievement.
VAISESHIKA PHILOSOPHY. Date of Sti.tr as.

IT is fortunate that with regard to the Yaiseshika philosophy, or rather with regard to the Vaiseshika-Sutras, we are able to fix a date below which their composition cannot be placed. In the year 1885 Professor Leumann, well known by his valuable researches in 6raina literature, published an article, ' The old reports on the schisms of the (rainas,3 in the Indische Studies,, XVII, pp. 91-135. Among the various heresies there mentioned, the sixth, we are told, p. 121, was founded by the author of the Vaisesiyasutta of the Chaulii race, and hence called Chauluga \ If there could be any doubt that this is meant for the Vaiseshika-Sutras it would at once be dispersed by the 144 so-called points of that system, as mentioned by the author, ffinabhadra. £inabhadra's date is fixed by Professor Leumann^in^the eighth century A.D., and is certainly not later. This, it is true, is -no great antiquity, still, if we consider the age of our Samkhya-Sutras, referred now to the thirteenth century A. p., even such a date, if only certain, would be worth^ having. But we can make another step backward. Haribhadra, originally a Brahman, but converted to Gainism, has left us a work called the ShacZdarsanasamu^aya-sutram, which contains a short abstract of the six Dar&unas in which the Vaiseshika-darsana is described as the sixth, and in that description likewise we meet with the most important technical terms of the Vaiseshika. This short but important text was published in the first volume of the Giornale della Societa Asiatica 1 Could this be meant for Auluka ?

DATES FROM TIBETAN SOURCES. 439 Italiana, 1887, and Sanskrit scholarship is greatly indebted to Professor C. Puini for this and other valuable contributions of his to (?aina literature. The author, Haribhadra, died in 1055 of the Vira-era, i.e. 585 Samvat, that is 528 A.D. This would give us an attestation for the Vaiseshika-Sutras as early as that of the Samkhya-karikas, if not earlier, and it is curious to observe that in Haribhadra's time the number six of the Darsanas was already firmly established. For, after describing the (i) Bauddha, (3) Naiyayika, (3) Samkhya, (4) Craina, (5) Vaiseshika, and (6) traiminiya systems, he remarks, that if some consider the Vaiseshika not altogether different from the Nyaya, there would be only five orthodox systems (Astika), but that in that case the number six could be completed by the Lokayita (sic) system which he proceeds to describe, but which, of course, is not an Astika, but a most decided Nastika system of philosophy. It is curious to observe that here again the Vedanta-philosophy, and the Yoga also, are passed over in silence by the Grainas, though, for reasons explained before, we have no right to conclude from this that these systems had at that time not yet been reduced to a systematic form like the other four Darsanas. What we learn from this passage is that early in the sixth century A.D, the Ny&ya, Samkhya, Vaiseshika, and PurvaMim£ims& systems of philosophy formed the subject of scientific study among the 6?ainas, and we may hope that a further search for Graina MSS. may bring us some new discoveries, and some further light on the chronological development of philosophical studies in India.
Dates from Tibetan Sources.

Whenever we shall know more of the sources from which Tibetan writers derived their information about Indian literary matters, more light may possibly come from thence on the dates of the Indian philosophical systems of thought also. It is true that the introduction of Buddhism into Tibet dates from the eighth century only, but the translators of Sanskrit originals, such as Santi Kakshita, Padma Sambhava, Dharmakirti, Dipamkara Sri#/?ana and others, may have been in possession of much earlier information, 44O INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

In an account1 of King Kanishka (85-106 A. D.) and his Great Council under Vasumitra and Pftrnaka, we read that there was at that time in Kashmir a Buddhist of the name of Sutra who maintained a large Buddhist congregation headed by a sage Dharmarakshita, and he is said to have belonged to the Vaiseshika school 2. This would prove the existence of the Vaiseshika philosophy in the first century A.D., a date so welcome that we must not allow ourselves to accept it till we know what authority there was for the Tibetan writers to adopt it. It is taken from Sumpahi Choijung, and the -same authority states that after the death of Kanishka, a rich householder of the name of Jati who lived at Asvaparanta in the north, invited Vasunetra, a monk of the Vaifieshika school, from . Maru in the west, and another, Gosha Samgha from Bactria, and supported the native clergy, consisting of three hundred thousand monks, for a period of ten years.
Kaw&da.

Although Ny&ya and Vaiseshika have been often treated as sister philosophies, we must, after having examined Gotama's philosophy, give, for the sake of completeness, at least a general outline of Ka^&da's system also. It does not contain much that is peculiar to it, and seems to presuppose much that we found already in the other systems. Even the theory of AT&US or atoms, generally cited as its peculiar character, was evidently known to the Nyaya, though it is more fully developed by the Vaiseshikas. "it begins with the usual promise of teaching something from which springs elevation or the summum bonum, and that something Ka-nada calls Dharma or merit. From a particular kind of merit springs, according to Ka^&da, true knowledge of certain Pad&rthas, or categories, and from this once more the summum bonum. These categories, of which we spoke before as part of the Ny&ya-philosophy, embrace the whole realm of knowledge, and are: (i) substance, Dravya; (2) quality, Gurai; (3) action, Karman; (4) genus or community, S&m&nya, or what constitutes 1 Journal of Buddhist Text Society, voL It p. i seq. 3 Ibid., vol I, part 3, p. 19.

QUALITIES. 441 a genus ; (5) species or particularity, Visesha, or what constitutes an individual ; (6) inhesion or inseparability, Samavaya ; (7) according to some, privation or negation, AbhAva. These are to be considered by means of their mutual similarities and dissimilarities, that is, by showing how they differ and how far they agree. Here we have, indeed, what comes much nearer to Aristotle's categories than Gotama's Padarthas. These categories or predicaments were believed to contain an enumeration of all things capable of being named, i.e. of being known. If the number of Aristotle's categories was controverted, no wonder that those of KaTiada should have met with the same fate. It has always been a moot point whether Abhava, nonexistence, deserves a place among them, while some philosophers were anxious to add two more, namely, Sakti, potentia, and Sadrisya, similitude.
Substances. I. The substances, accordingA to the Vaiseshikas, are:
zh: （5）結論（Nigamana），因此它有火。在兩種情況下，推理的過程都是相同的，但第二種被認為更具修辭性、更具說服力，因此在論辯中更加有用。
安南巴哈（Annambhaa）所謂的「自己的結論」（conclusion for oneself），逐字相應於亞里斯多德三段論的第一種形式：—
凡是冒煙的都是火熱的，山冒煙；
因此，這座山是火焰般的。
然而，我們不能忘記，無論這些簡短的摘錄中包含了多少形式邏輯，對於喬答摩而言，其唯一的目的乃是將知識描述為自我的一種性質，而由於這種知識並不侷限於感覺知覺，喬答摩認為有必要解釋推理性知識的本質，並證明其合理性。這與其說是邏輯1， 1 《尼耶亞經》（Nyaya-Sutras）I, 32。
《勝論經》第九卷，2，4。
八、瓦伊舍什卡（Yaiseshika）的術語為：（一）Pratigrna，（二）Apadesa，（三）Nidarsana，（四）Amisawdhana，（五）Pratyamnaya。

正如阿努馬那（*anumaana*）對於卡那達（Kanada）來說乃是理性知識一般。他明確意識到歸納推理與演繹推理的不可分割性。從亞里斯多德時代至今，形式邏輯學家對人類心智機制採取純粹的技術性關注，他蒐集、整理和分析我們推理能力的功能，如其在觀察中所呈現者。然而占據喬達摩（Gotama）心思的問題乃是：我們如何能認識任何我們不能、實則根本無法通過感官知覺的東西；換言之，我們如何能為推論知識辯護。從這一觀點看來，我們不難發現，無論是歸納法抑或演繹法，若各自為政，都無法充分滿足他的需求。演繹推理本身雖然在形成遍充（*Vyaptis*）時可能極其有用，它可以給予我們的知識以各種不同的面向，但它永遠無法有所增益。因此，一方面喬達摩不能依賴演繹法，因為它傳授不了新的東西；另一方面他也不能完全依靠歸納法，因為它無法教導任何確定的或無條件的事物。

根據喬達摩（Gotama）的主張，一切知識的唯一對象是絕對真理或正量（Prama）。他與亞里士多德一樣深知，為了證明全稱（ὁλον），歸納法（ἐπαγωγή）必須是遍歷一切個例（διὰ πάντων），而這是不可能的。通過歸納推理獲得的知識，嚴格來說，始終只能是關於個別情形（ἐπὶ τὸ ποιόν），而非喬達摩所謂的正量（Prama）。亞里士多德通過歸納法所得出的結論——例如膽汁少的動物長壽——可稱為周遍關係（Vyapti）。他的論證方式是：人、馬和騾（C）是長壽的（A）；人、馬和騾（C）膽汁少（B）；因此所有膽汁少的動物都是長壽的。喬達摩在此方面差異不大，但他的表述方式有所不同。他會說，凡我們看到膽汁少的屬性之處，也看到長壽的屬性，例如在人、馬、騾等身上。但他不會止步於此。他僅將此周遍關係（Vyapti）視為確立新法則的手段；他會將其作為演繹的工具，並說：「既然我們知道象膽汁少，因此我們也知道象是長壽的。」或舉另一例，當亞里士多德說所有人都是必死的時，喬達摩會如此表述……
人們會說人類受死亡所普遍浸透，或者說我們從未見過不具死亡性的人類；而亞里斯多德得出結論，國王是有死亡性的，因為他們屬於人的階級，喬達摩如果只是為自己立論，而非為他人立論，則會說國王身分被人性所普遍浸透，人性又被死亡性所普遍浸透，因此國王是有死亡性的。

對於這種推理方式提出異議是容易的，而我們將看到印度哲學家本身並未遲遲不提出這些異議，同樣也迅速作出了回應。有一點可以說是有利於印度方法的。如果我們繼續積累個案來進行歸納，如在上述情形中那樣，我們添加馬、騾子、人類等等，我們固然日漸逼近一條普遍規則，但我們永遠不能排除所有實際的例外，更不用說排除所有可能的例外。反之，印度人則是說：「凡我們見過膽汁少這個特性之處，我們都觀察到長壽」，或者更好地說：「我們從未見過長壽而不具膽汁少這個特性的」，然後提供若干純粹個案，而這些個案僅作為例證，藉此排除例外的現實性，雖然不排除其可能性。他陳述為事實，凡一者現身之處，他者亦被觀察到現身，從而將關於任何相反情形之舉證責任轉移到對方肩上。印度人對歸納法的本質瞭解得足夠清楚，足以用歐洲哲學家的措辭說出：僅因在九十九個案例中一個*Vyapti*（規則）碰巧為真，不能推出它在第一百個案例中亦會如此。然而，如果能被證明，煙從未在不伴隨火的情況下被見過，那麼煙與火的相互內在性與不可分割的聯繫，就會比透過任何數量的兩者被見在一起的實際累積個案而建立得更加穩固。
在形成周遍（Vyapti）的條件下——也就是說，在形成普遍規則的條件下——條件（Upadhis）大大佔據了印度哲學家的思想。
抱歉，我注意到您提供的英文原文似乎包含梵文音譯和訛誤字符，使其難以辨識為標準英文。這段文字看起來像是梵文哲學著作的引文或題註，而非穆勒1873年《宗教學導論》的英文原文。 為了提供準確的翻譯，我需要您提供： 1. **清晰的英文原文**（確認非梵文或其他語言的音譯） 2. 或該文本的出處確認（版本、頁碼） 若您能提供穆勒著作中確實的英文段落，我將按照您的規則進行專業翻譯。
**論他人之推論** 435 在這一主題上已經著述繁卷累牘，雖然這些著作未必能為普遍理性的起源問題投射任何新的光芒，但它們無論如何都為歐洲哲學家在捍衛歸納推理與演繹推理兩方面所做的努力提供了一個耐人尋味的平行對照。

現在似乎還不到時候，可以開始批評印度哲學家所闡述的歸納法與演繹法。我們必須先更充分地瞭解它們。迄今為止所提出的各種異議，對於喬達摩（Gotama）與迦那陀（Kanada）本人來說，決非陌生之物。依據他們的先論（Pftrvapaksha）與後論（Uttarapaksha）系統，凡是可以想見的異議，他們都加以提出，並仔細分析和駁答。因此，歐洲哲學家已經指出：命題「凡有煙處即有火」，會因引入「如同廚房爐灶上」這樣的例證，而實際上喪失其普遍性。但是印度邏輯學家也完全清楚，這一例證並非三段論法的本質要素。他們單純視例證為論辯目的上的有益提示，作為輔助記憶的說明，而非證明過程本身的必要組成部分。其目的是提醒我們必須尋求所見煙與所隱含（而未見）火之間的遍在性（Vyapti）。因此，唯在修辭三段論法或為他人而作的三段論法中，例證才有其適當的地位。在喬達摩的《經》（Sfttra）第一卷第三十五條中說：「第三項或例證是某個該事實的熟悉情況，由於它具有某種必然伴隨所欲確立者之特性，故得以確立（與理由相結合）該欲確立之特性的存在。」因此，責任在於印度修辭學，而遠甚於印度邏輯學，才導致了包含這個有問題例證的第三項之引入；雖然修辭學並非邏輯學，然而如惠特利（Whately）所言，它乃邏輯學的分支。
1 里特爾（Ritter）《哲學史》第四卷，第365頁，指出「迦那陀論證中有兩項顯然多餘，而第三項若引入例證，其結論的普遍性即遭破壞」。Ff 2 436 印度哲學。

事實上，喬答摩（Gotama）對於形成**周遍性**（Vyapti, pervasion）的關心，遠甚於它如何日後可作為三段論之基礎的方式——而這必然取決於周遍性的性質。在喬答摩學派中，周遍性被視為三類，即**順逆周遍**（Anvaya-vyatireki）、**順周遍**（Kevalanvayi）與**逆周遍**（Kevala-vyatireki）。第一類——順逆周遍——呈現與不呈現，如下例所示：有煙處有火，無火處無煙。第二類，即順周遍，亦即僅呈現者，如下例所示：凡可認知者皆可命名，其中不可能提出任何非可認知之物。第三類，即逆周遍，如下例所示：土異於其他元素，因為它具有香氣。於此我們無法進而言，凡異於其他元素者皆具香氣，因為相應之唯一個例（Udaharana）會復為土。然而我們必須言，不異於其他元素者不具香氣，如水（本身）。然而此土並非如此，非無香氣，因此它並非不異於其他元素，而是異於它們，得證。

印度哲學家們也對Upādhis（條件）的運作——即賦予Vyāpti的條件——投入了大量關注。因此在普通的Vyāpti中，山上有煙是因為有火，濕潤燃料的存在就是一個Upādhi，或者說不可或缺的條件。這個Upādhi遍佈應被確立者（Sādhya-vyāpaka），在此例中即火，但它並不遍佈確立者（Sādhana-vyāpaka），亦即煙，因為火並非被濕潤燃料所遍佈，或者說並非總是伴隨於濕潤燃料——例如在熾熱的鐵球的情況下，我們確實有火但沒有煙。因此不能必然推斷有煙就必有火，或無煙就必無火。印度思想在這些推理細節上究竟能走多遠，可以從巴蘭泰因博士（Dr. Ballantyne）在其《紐亞耶哲學講座》中給出的例子看出，該書主要根據《推理集成》（Tarkasamgraha）編纂，第59頁：——

本譯本的功勞並非源自於我，而是歸功於已故的巴蘭泰因博士，他得到了貝納雷斯梵文學院的尼夜耶學者（Nyaya-Pandits）的協助，才得以理清尼夜耶邏輯這些蜘蛛網般的複雜思路。若要完成如此艱鉅的任務，本地學者的協助似乎幾乎是不可或缺的。
瓦依舍詩卡哲學‧斯蒂特拉經的年代

關於瑜伽奢沙卡（Yaiseshika）哲學，或毋寧說關於《吠世史迦經》（Vaiseshika-Sutras），我們幸運地得以確定其成書下限。一八八五年，以在耆那教文獻研究中卓有成就而聞名的勒烏曼（Leumann）教授在《印度研究》（Indische Studies）第十七卷第九十一至一三五頁發表了一篇文章〈關於耆那教分派的古代記載〉（The old reports on the schisms of the Gainas）。在該文所述的各種異端中，第六種據稱（第一二一頁）是由喬盧提族（Chaulii）的《吠世史迦經》（Vaisesiyasutta）作者創立的，因此被稱為喬盧迦派（Chauluga）。如果有人懷疑這是否指的就是《吠世史迦經》，那麼該系統所謂的一百四十四個論點（據作者金那跋陀羅﹝£inabhadra﹞所述）立即就能消除此疑。勒烏曼教授確定金那跋陀羅的年代在西元八世紀，肯定不晚於此。誠然，這並非太古遠的年代，但若考慮到我們的《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）如今被推至西元十三世紀，縱然如此確定的年代也值得重視。但我們還可以再往前推進一步。哈里跋陀羅（Haribhadra）原為婆羅門，後皈依耆那教，著有《六派要義論經》（Shaddarsanasamu°aya-sutram）一書，內容簡述六派哲學（Darsanas），其中將吠世史迦派（Vaiseshika-darsana）列為第六派，在該論述中我們同樣遇見《吠世史迦》的最重要的技術用語。這份簡短但重要的文獻曾在《亞洲學會雜誌》（Giornale della Societa Asiatica）第一卷發表。這是否可能指的就是奧盧迦（Auluka）呢？

來自藏文文獻的年代記載。439 義大利文本，1887 年出版，梵文學術研究在很大程度上受惠於普尼教授(C. Puini)為耆那教文獻所做的這項及其他寶貴貢獻。作者哈里跋陀羅(Haribhadra)卒於毘羅紀年(Vira-era)的 1055 年，即薩姆瓦特(Samvat)585 年，相當於西元 528 年。若非更早的話，這將為我們提供《勝論經》(Vaiseshika-Sutras)與《數論頌》(Samkhya-karikas)同等早期的證據，而有趣的是，在哈里跋陀羅的時代，六派哲學(Darsanas)的數目已經確定。因為在描述(1)佛教(Bauddha)、(2)尼夜耶派(Naiyayika)、(3)數論派(Samkhya)、(4)耆那教(Craina)、(5)勝論派(Vaiseshika)及(6)獨立派(traiminiya)體系之後，他評論道，若有人認為勝論派與尼夜耶派並無本質差異，則正統派系(Astika)將僅有五個；但在這種情況下，第六個位置可由他隨後描述的順世派(Lokayita)體系補足——儘管這當然不是正統派(Astika)，而是極為決然的非正統派(Nastika)哲學體系。同樣有趣的是，此處耆那教徒再次對韋丹塔哲學及瑜伽派保持沉默，但基於前面已說明的理由，我們無權因此推斷這些體系在當時尚未如其他四派那樣被整理成系統形式。我們從這段文獻所學到的是，西元六世紀初，尼夜耶派、數論派、勝論派及前彌明撒派(Purva-Mimamsa)哲學體系已成為耆那教徒進行學術研究的主題，我們可期待進一步搜索耆那教手稿將為我們帶來新的發現，以及關於印度哲學研究編年發展的進一步線索。
西藏文獻所載之年代
一旦我們對藏族作者有關印度文學事務之資訊來源有更深入的認識，那麼也許能從中為印度哲學思想體系的年代學問題投射更多的光芒。誠然，佛教傳入西藏的時期僅可追溯至八世紀，但梵文原典的譯者，例如聖蒂‧卡克希塔（Santi Kakshita）、蓮花生（Padma Sambhava）、法稱（Dharmakirti）、燃燈智（Dipamkara Sri#/?ana）及其他人士，都可能掌有遠更古老的文獻資料。

在關於迦膩色迦王（西元八五─一○六年）及其在瓦蘇密多與毗羅那迦主持下的大議會的記載中，我們讀到當時在喀什米爾有一位名叫蘇陀的佛教徒，他主持著一個龐大的佛教教團，由智者達摩護持領導，據說他屬於勝論學派¹。這將證明勝論哲學在西元一世紀時的存在，這個時間點令人欣喜，但我們在接受它之前必須先了解西藏作者採納此說有何根據。此說取自《蘇婆波底藏經》，同一權威文獻又記載，在迦膩色迦王去世後，住在北方阿濕波蘭多的一位名叫闍提的富有居士，從西方馬魯邀來屬於勝論學派的僧人瓦蘇涅多，以及來自巴克特里亞的高沙僧伽，並供養本地僧團三十萬名僧侶達十年之久。
考陀（Kawd&da）。

雖然紐亞（Nyāya）與瓦伊舍什迦（Vaiseshika）經常被視為姐妹哲學，但在檢視了喬答摩（Gotama）的哲學之後，為求完整起見，我們必須至少對迦拿陀（Kaṇāda）的體系提出一般性的概要。該體系所包含的獨特之處並不甚多，且似乎預設了我們在其他體系中已經發現的諸多內容。甚至被普遍引用為其獨特特徵的原子論（Anu）理論，顯然紐亞派已知悉，雖然瓦伊舍什迦派對其有更充分的發展。¹ 它以慣常的承諾開始，承諾傳授某種至高福祉之源的教說，而迦拿陀將這種東西稱為法（Dharma）或功德。根據迦拿陀的說法，從特定種類的功德便產生出對某些範疇（Padārtha）的真正知識，而這些範疇就是：真知識，而從真知識再度產生至高福祉。² 這些範疇（我們之前曾作為紐亞派哲學的一部分而提及）涵蓋整個知識領域，其為：（1）實體，德拉維亞（Dravya）；（2）性質，古那（Guṇa）；（3）行為，業（Karman）；（4）共相或普遍性，薩摩紐亞（Sāmānya），或者構成 ¹ *Journal of Buddhist Text Society*, vol. II, p. 1 seq. ² *Ibid.*, vol. I, part 3, p. 19.

**品質** 441 一個屬（genus）；（5）種或特殊性，Visesha，或構成個體的內容；（6）內住或不可分離性，Samavaya；（7）根據某些論述，缺乏或否定，Abhava。這些應當通過它們相互之間的相似性與差異性來考察，即通過展示它們如何相異以及相同的程度來理解。在這裡，我們確實發現了更接近於亞里士多德的範疇而非高茶摩之帕達爾塔（Padarthas）的東西。這些範疇或謂詞類被認為包含了所有能夠被命名的事物，亦即能夠被認知的事物的列舉。如果亞里士多德的範疇數目本身就有爭議，那麼卡尼亞達（Kañiada）的範疇遭遇同樣的命運就不足為奇了。缺乏或非存在性（Abhava）是否應當在其中佔一席之地，向來是個有爭論的問題，而某些哲學家則急欲再添加兩個範疇，即能力（Sakti, potentia）與相似性（Sadrisya, similitude）。
物質。一、根據瓦伊舍什卡派（Vaiseshikas）的說法，物質包括：
